questions.question: The domain of the function $\sqrt {\cos x - } 1$ is
questions.question_raw: The domain of the function {tex}\sqrt {\cos x - } 1{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
This function exists only if
This function exists only if
$\cos x-1 \geq 0$
$\Rightarrow \cos x \geq 1$
$\Rightarrow \cos x>1$
OR
$\cos x = 1$ 
since maximum value of cosine function is 1 so, cos x >1 is not possible
so, 
 $\cos x = 1$
$\cos x=\cos 2 n \pi$
$\Rightarrow x=2 n \pi(n \in I)$

questions.question: The domain of the function f(x) = $ \sqrt {\left| x \right| - x} $ is
questions.question_raw: The domain of the function f(x) = {tex} \sqrt {\left| x \right| - x} {/tex} is
questions.explanation:
f(x) = $ \sqrt {\left| x \right| - x} $ 
This function exists only if |x| - x $ \ge$ 0
$ \Rightarrow $ |x| $ \ge$ x
$\forall $ $x \in R$ it is true 
So domain of f(x) is R.

questions.question: The domain of the function f(x) = $ \sqrt {1 - \cos x} $ is
questions.question_raw: The domain of the function f(x) = {tex} \sqrt {1 - \cos x} {/tex} is
questions.explanation:
This function exists only if
1 - cos x $\ge$ 0
$\Rightarrow$ cos x $\le$ 1
It is possible $\forall x \in R$

questions.question: The domain of the function f(x) = $\sqrt {\sin x - 1} $ is
questions.question_raw: The domain of the function f(x) = {tex}\sqrt {\sin x - 1} {/tex} is
questions.explanation:
This exists only if
sin x - 1 $\ge$ 0
sin x $\ge$ 1 
sin x > 1, not possible
$\therefore$ sin x = 1
$\Rightarrow$ sin x = sin $\left(2 n \pi+\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $2 n \pi+\frac{\pi}{2}(n \in I)$

questions.question: Let x be any real, then [x + y] = [x] + [y] holds for
questions.question_raw: Let x be any real, then [x + y] = [x] + [y] holds for
questions.explanation:
[x] = x $\forall$ x $\in$ I
So,
[x + y] = [x] + [y] only if x and y $\in$ I

questions.question_raw: The function f(x) = x + cos x

options.label: A
options.text: none of tis defined for all imaginary

questions.explanation:
f(x) = x; g(x) = cos x 
D_f = R
D_g = R
D_f+g = D_f $\cap$ D_g
So,
D_f+g = R
So given function is defined for all reals.

questions.question: If f(x) = $\frac{1}{\sqrt{x-|x|}}$, then D_f is equal to
questions.question_raw: If f(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{x-|x|}}{/tex}, then Df is equal to
questions.explanation:
This function is defined only if 
x - |x| > 0
$\Rightarrow$ x > |x|
Which is not possible because
|x| $\ge$ x
so, $x \in \phi $

questions.question: If f(x) = tan x, $-\frac{\pi}{2}<x<\frac{\pi}{2}$ and g(x) = $\sqrt{3-x^2}$, then domain of the function gof is
questions.question_raw: If f(x) = tan x, {tex}-\frac{\pi}{2}<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and g(x) = {tex}\sqrt{3-x^2}{/tex}, then domain of the function gof is
questions.explanation:
gof(x) = g(f(x))
Which is defined only if
3 - tan^2 x $\geq$ 0
$\Rightarrow$ 3 $\geq$ tan^2 x
$\Rightarrow$ -$\sqrt{3}$ $\leq$ tan x $\leq$ $\sqrt{3}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $-\frac{\pi}{3} \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{3}$ 
So domain of gof is $\left[ { - \frac{\pi }{3},\frac{\pi }{3}} \right]$

questions.question: If f (x) = tan x, $-\frac{\pi}{2}<x<\frac{\pi}{2}$ and g (x) = $\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} $ , then domain of fog is
questions.question_raw: If f (x) = tan x, {tex}-\frac{\pi}{2}<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and g (x) = {tex}\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} {/tex} , then domain of fog is
questions.explanation:
fog(x) = f(g(x)) = $f(\sqrt{1-x^{2}})$
$\Rightarrow$ fog(x) = tan $\sqrt{1-x^{2}}$
and it is defined if
1 - x^2 $\geq$ 0
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 $\leq$ 1
$\Rightarrow$ -1 $\leq$ x $\leq$ 1

questions.question: If f(x) =$\sqrt { - x} $, then domain of fof is
questions.question_raw: If f(x) ={tex}\sqrt { - x} {/tex}, then domain of fof is
questions.explanation:
$\sqrt {f(x)} $ is defined only if
f(x) will be non negative.
Hence $ \sqrt { - f(x)} {\rm{ }}$is defined only for 0.

questions.question: If y = tan $\sqrt {{\frac \pi 9} - {x^2}} $ then
questions.question_raw: If y = tan {tex}\sqrt {{\frac \pi 9} - {x^2}} {/tex} then
questions.explanation:
Let $z = \sqrt {\frac{\pi }{9} - {x^2}} $ and it is defined if 
$\frac{\pi}{9}-x^{2} \geq 0$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 $\leq$ $\frac{\pi}{9}$ 
$\therefore 0 \leq z \leq \frac{\pi }{3}$ 
$\therefore 0 \leq \tan z \leq \sqrt 3 $

questions.question: The interval in which the values of f(x) lie where f(x) = 3 sin $\sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{16}-x^2}$ is
questions.question_raw: The interval in which the values of f(x) lie where f(x) = 3 sin {tex}\sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{16}-x^2}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
z = $\sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2}}{16}-x^{2}}$ 
$\therefore$ $0 \leq z \leq \frac{\pi}{4}$ 
$\therefore$ 0 $\leq$ sin z $\leq$ $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ 
0 $\leq$ 3 sin z $\leq$ $\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}$

questions.question: The range of the function f(x) = $\frac{x^2-3 x+2}{x^2+x-6}$ is
questions.question_raw: The range of the function f(x) = {tex}\frac{x^2-3 x+2}{x^2+x-6}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Let, y = $\frac{x^{2}-3 x+2}{x^{2}+x-6}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ y(x^2 + x - 6) = x^2 - 3x + 2
$\Rightarrow$ (y - 1)x^2 + (y + 3)x - 6y - 2 = 0
x = $\frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c}}{2 a}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{-(y+3) \pm \sqrt{(y+3)^{2}-4(y-1)(-6 y-2)}}{2(y-1)}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{-(y+3) \pm \sqrt{25 y^{2}-10 y+1}}{2(y-1)}$
Now for x to be defined, y - 1 $\ne$ 0 $\Rightarrow$ y $\ne$ 1
Also, 
25y^2 - 10y + 1 $\ge$ 0
$\Rightarrow$ (5y - 1)^2 $\ge$ 0
But domain of f(x): x $\ne$ 2, -3
But, 
When, y = $\frac{1}{5}$, x = 2
So, range = R - {1, $\frac{1}{5}$}

questions.question: If f(x) = log$\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)$, then $f\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}\right)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If f(x) = log{tex}\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right){/tex}, then {tex}f\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}\right){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
f(x) = $\log \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)$
$f\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}\right)=\log \left(\frac{1+\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}}{1-\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}}\right)$
$=\log \left(\frac{1+x^2+2 x}{1+x^2-2 x}\right)=\log \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)^2$
$=2 \log \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)=2 f(x)$

questions.question: The domain of the real-valued function $f(x)=\frac{(x-3)(x-1)}{\sqrt{x^2-4}}$ is
questions.question_raw: The domain of the real-valued function {tex}f(x)=\frac{(x-3)(x-1)}{\sqrt{x^2-4}}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
For f(x) to be real, we much have,
${{x^2}\; - \;4\; > 0}$ 
${ \Rightarrow (x - 2)(x + 2) > 0}$ 
${ \Rightarrow - \infty < - 2\;or\;2 < x < \infty }$ 
$\therefore$ Domain ${ = \;( - \infty , - 2) \cup (2,\infty )}$ ($\infty$ means infinity)

questions.question: A condition for a function y = f(x) to have an inverse is that it should be,
questions.question_raw: A condition for a function y = f(x) to have an inverse is that it should be,

options.label: B
options.text: strictly monotone and continuous in the domain

correct_option_texts: strictly monotone and continuous in the domain
questions.explanation: By theorem, A continuous and strictly monotonic function is invertible and the inverse function is also continuous.

questions.question: Find the number of injective functions from set A with elements to set B with 4 elements.
questions.question_raw: Find the number of injective functions from set A with elements to set B with 4 elements.
questions.explanation:
We know that if A and B are two sets having m and n elements respectively such that ${m \leqslant n,}$ then total number of injective from A to B is ${^n{{\text{C}}_{\text{m}}} \times {\text{m}}!}$ 
Here, n = 4, m = 3
$\therefore$ no. of injective =$ {\;^4}{{\text{C}}_3}\; \times 3! = \;4 \times 6 $ = 24

questions.question: The range of the function f(x) = $\frac{x^2-x+1}{x^2+x+1}$ is
questions.question_raw: The range of the function f(x) = {tex}\frac{x^2-x+1}{x^2+x+1}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Let y = $ \;\frac{{{{\text{x}}^2} - {\text{x}} + 1}}{{{{\text{x}}^2} + {\text{x}} + 1}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ y (x^2 + x + 1) = x^2 - x + 1 
$\Rightarrow $ (y - 1)x^2 + (y + 1)x + y - 1 = 0
For x to be real, we must have
(y + 1)^2 - 4(y - 1)^2 $\ge$ 0 (Discriminant = b^2 - 4ac) 
$\Rightarrow$ -3y^2 + 10y - 3 $\ge$ 0 $\Rightarrow$ 3y^2 - 10y + 3 $\le$ 0
$\Rightarrow$ (3y - 1)(y - 3) $\le$ 0
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{1}{3} \leq y \leq 3$ $\Rightarrow$ y $\in$ $\left[\frac{1}{3}, 3\right]$ 
$\therefore$ Range ${ = \left[ {\frac{1}{3},\;3} \right]}$

questions.question: On the set Z of all integers define f : Z $ \to $ Z as follows : f(x) = $\frac{x}{2}$ if x is even, and f(x) = 0 if x is odd, then f is
questions.question_raw: On the set Z of all integers define f : Z {tex} \to {/tex} Z as follows : f(x) = {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex} if x is even, and f(x) = 0 if x is odd, then f is
questions.explanation:
we have, f : z $\rightarrow$ z defined by,
${\left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\frac{x}{2},\;if\;x\;is\;even} \\ {0,\;if\;x\;is\;odd} \end{array}} \right.}$ 
One - One: - Here f(1) = 0, f(3) = 0
$\therefore$ 1 $\ne$ 3 $\Rightarrow$ f(1) = f(3)
f is one - one.
Onto: Let ${y \in z}$ be arbitrary
If y is even then there exists an even number ${2y \in z}$ such that
f(2y) = $ \frac{{2y}}{2} $ = y
If y is odd then there exists an even number ${2y \in z}$ such that
f(2y) = $\frac{{2y}}{2}$ = y 
f is onto.

questions.question: Let f : R $ \to $ R be defined by f(x) = 3x – 4, then f^-1(x) is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let f : R {tex} \to {/tex} R be defined by f(x) = 3x – 4, then f-1(x) is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let y = f(x) = 3x - 4, then
y = 3x - 4
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{y+4}{3}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ f^-1(y) = $\frac{y+4}{3}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ f^-1 (x) = $\frac{x+4}{3}$

questions.question: If $A = \{ (x,y):{x^2} + {y^2} = 25\} $ and $B = \{ (x,y):{x^2} + 9{y^2} + {y^2} = 144\} ,$ then $A \cap B$ contains
questions.question_raw: If {tex}A = \{ (x,y):{x^2} + {y^2} = 25\} {/tex} and {tex}B = \{ (x,y):{x^2} + 9{y^2} + {y^2} = 144\} ,{/tex} then {tex}A \cap B{/tex} contains
questions.explanation:
We will solve equations in A and B simultaneously and find values of x and y. The no. of possible ordered pairs from these values will be elements in ${A \cap B.}$
Now, From B,${{x^2} + 9{y^2} + {y^2} = \;144}$ and 
From ${A,\;{x^2} + {y^2}\; = \;25}$
${\therefore 9{y^2} + 25\; = \;144\; \Rightarrow 9{y^2}\; = 119}$
${ \Rightarrow y\; = \; \pm \sqrt {\frac{{119}}{9}} }$
${\therefore {x^2} + {y^2} = 25\; \Rightarrow {x^2}\; = 25 = \frac{{119}}{9}\; = \;\frac{{106}}{9}}$
${ \Rightarrow x\; = \; \pm \sqrt {\frac{{106}}{9}} }$
$\therefore$ x has two value, y has two values
$\therefore$ possible ordered pairs = 4
$\therefore$ ${A \cap B}$ has 4 elements

questions.question: If f(x) = $\frac{x}{x-1}=\frac{1}{y}$, then f(y) =
questions.question_raw: If f(x) = {tex}\frac{x}{x-1}=\frac{1}{y}{/tex}, then f(y) =
questions.explanation:
We have, f(x) = $\frac{x}{{x - 1}} = \frac{1}{y}$ 
$\therefore$ y = $ \frac{{x - 1}}{x}$ 
Now, f(x) = $ \frac{x}{{x - 1}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ f(y) = $\frac{y}{y-1}=\frac{\frac{x-1}{z}}{\frac{x-1}{x}-1}=\frac{x-1}{x-1-x}$ = $\frac{x-1}{-1}$ = 1 - x

questions.question: If f (x + y + z) = f (x) f (y ) f (z) for all x , y z and if f (2) = 4, f’ (0) = 5 and f (0) $ \ne $0, then f’ (2) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If f (x + y + z) = f (x) f (y ) f (z) for all x , y z and if f (2) = 4, f’ (0) = 5 and f (0) {tex} \ne {/tex}0, then f’ (2) is equal to
questions.explanation:
We know that if ${{\text{f}}({\text{x}} + {\text{y}} + {\text{z}})\; = \;{\text{f}}({\text{x}}){\text{f}}({\text{y}}){\text{f}}({\text{z}}),}$ then
${{\text{f}}({\text{x}})\; = \;{{\text{a}}^{{\text{kx}}}}}$, a and k being constants
Now, ${{\text{f}}(2)\; = \;{{\text{a}}^{2{\text{k}}}}\; \Rightarrow \;{{({{\text{a}}^{\text{k}}})}^2}\; = {2^2}\; \Rightarrow \;{{\text{a}}^{\text{k}}}\; = \pm 2}$
${\therefore \;{\text{f}}({\text{x}})\; = \;{{({{\text{a}}^{\text{k}}})}^{\text{x}}}\; = \;{{( \pm \;2)}^{\text{x}}}}$
${\therefore \;{\text{f'}}({\text{x}})\; = \;{{(2)}^{\text{x}}}{{\log }_{\text{e}}}2}$ (as negative log is not defined)
${ \Rightarrow \;{\text{f'}}(0)\; = \;({{\log }_{\text{e}}}2)\; \Rightarrow \;5\; = \;{{\log }_{\text{e}}}2}$
${\therefore \;{\text{f'}}({\text{x}})\; = \;{{(2)}^{\text{x}}}.5\; \Rightarrow {\text{f'}}(2)\; = {{(2)}^2}\;.5\; = \;20}$

questions.question: The domain of the function $\frac{1}{[x]}+\sqrt{2 x-x^2}$ is
questions.question_raw: The domain of the function {tex}\frac{1}{[x]}+\sqrt{2 x-x^2}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
We have, f(x) = $ \frac{1}{{\left[ x \right]}} + \sqrt {2x - {x^2}}$ 
For f(x) to be real, x $\in$ [0, 1] and 2x - x^2 = 0 
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ [0, 1] and x^2 - 2x = 0
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ [0, 1] and x(x - 2) = 0 
$\Rightarrow$ x $\notin$ [0, 1] and x = 0, 2
$\therefore$ Domain = [1, 2]

questions.question: If A = {(x, y) : x^2 + y^2 = 5} and B = {(x, y) : 2x = 5y}, then A $\cap$ B contains
questions.question_raw: If A = {(x, y) : x2 + y2 = 5} and B = {(x, y) : 2x = 5y}, then A {tex}\cap{/tex} B contains
questions.explanation:
From A, x^2 + y^2 = 5 and from B, 2x = 5y
Now, 2x = 5y $\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{5}{2 y}$ 
$\therefore$ x^2 + y^2 = 5 $\Rightarrow$ $\left(\frac{5}{2 y}\right)^{2}$ + y^2 = 5
$\Rightarrow$ 29y^2 = 20 $\Rightarrow$ y = $\pm \sqrt{\frac{20}{29}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 29y^2 = 20 $\Rightarrow$ y = $\pm \sqrt{\frac{20}{29}}$ 
$\therefore$ x = $\frac{5}{2}(\pm \sqrt{\frac{20}{29}})$ 
$\therefore$ Possible ordered pairs = four
But two ordered pair in which c is positive and y is negative will be rejected as it will not be satisifed by the equation in B. Therefore,
A $ \cap$ B contains 2 elements.

questions.question: If f : R $ \to $ R is given by f(x) = |x| and A = {x $\in$ R : x < 0} , then f^-1(A) equals
questions.question_raw: If f : R {tex} \to {/tex} R is given by f(x) = |x| and A = {x {tex}\in{/tex} R : x < 0} , then f-1(A) equals
questions.explanation:
Here, A = {x $\in$ R : x < 0} $\subseteq$ co-domain
f^-1(A) Contains those elements in R(domain) whose image is negative.
Since, f(x) = |x|
$\therefore$ no image of any elements of R(domain) is negative
$\therefore$ f^-1(A) = $\phi$

questions.question: If f : N $ \times $ N $ \to $N is such that f(m, n) = m + n where N is the set of natural number, then which of the following is true?
questions.question_raw: If f : N {tex} \times {/tex} N {tex} \to {/tex}N is such that f(m, n) = m + n where N is the set of natural number, then which of the following is true?

options.label: A
options.text: f is one-one but not onto

options.label: C
options.text: f is neither one-one nor onto

options.label: D
options.text: f is onto but not one-one

correct_option_texts: f is neither one-one nor onto
questions.explanation:
One - One: f(1, 2) = 1 + 2 = 3; f(2, 1) = 2 + 1
$\therefore$ f is not one - one
onto: f(1, 1) = 2, f(1, 2) = 3, f(2, 1) = 3
f(1, 3) = 4 = f(3, 1) and so on
$\therefore$ range of f = {2, 3, 4, 5, ....} $\ne$ N
$\therefore$ f is not onto.

questions.question: The function sin $\left( {\sin {\frac x 3}} \right)$ is periodic with period
questions.question_raw: The function sin {tex}\left( {\sin {\frac x 3}} \right){/tex} is periodic with period
questions.explanation:
Let f(x) = sin(sin($\frac {x}{3}$))
Period of sin x = 2$\pi$
Period of sin kx = $\frac {2\pi}{|k|}$
= $\frac {2\pi}{(\frac {1}{3})}$
= 6$\pi$

questions.question: The function f(x) = ${10^x}$ from R to [0, $\infty $) is
questions.question_raw: The function f(x) = {tex}{10^x}{/tex} from R to [0, {tex}\infty {/tex}) is
questions.explanation:
We have, ${f:R \to \left[ {0,\omega } \right]}$ defined by,
${f(x) = \;{{10}^x}}$
one - one: ${f({x_1})\; = \;f({x_2})}$
${ \Rightarrow \;{{10}^{{x_1}}}\; = {{10}^{{x_2}}}\; \Rightarrow \;{x_1}\; = \;{x_2}}$
$\therefore$ f is one - one
onto: Here, ${0 \in \left[ {0,\omega } \right]}$ (Co - domain)
Now, ${f(x)\; = \;0\; \Rightarrow \;{{10}^x}\; = \;0}$
But this is not possible for ${x \in R}$
$\therefore$ 0 does not has pre - image
$\therefore$ f is not onto

questions.question: For all x $ \in $ (0, 1)
questions.question_raw: For all x {tex} \in {/tex} (0, 1)
questions.explanation:
Let f(x) = x - log(1 + x) in [0, x]; x $ \in$ (0, 1) clearly, f is continuous on [0, x] and differentiable on (0, x).
Therefore by Lagrange's mean value theorem, there exists c $ \in$ (0, x) such that,
f'(c) = $ \;\frac{{{\text{f}}({\text{x}})\; - \;{\text{f}}(0)}}{{{\text{x}} - 0}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 1 - $\frac{1}{c}$ = $\frac{x-\log (1+x)-0}{x}$ {$\because$ f'(x) = 1 - $\frac{1}{1+x}$}
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{x-\log (1+x)}{x}$ = 1 - $\frac{1}{1+c}$ $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{x-\log (1+x)}{x}$ > 0 [$\because$ c $\in$ (0, 1) $\Rightarrow$ 1 - $\frac{1}{1+c}$ > 0]
$\Rightarrow$ x - log (1 + x) > 0 [$\because$ x $\in$ (0, 1)]
$\Rightarrow$ log (1 + x) < x

questions.question: The domain of the function $f(x) = \sqrt {x - 1} + \sqrt {6 - x} $
questions.question_raw: The domain of the function {tex}f(x) = \sqrt {x - 1} + \sqrt {6 - x} {/tex}
questions.explanation:
For f(x) to be real, we must have,
${x - 1 \geqslant 0\;and\;6 - x \geqslant 0}$ 
${ \Rightarrow \;x \geqslant \varphi \;and\;x - 6\; \leqslant 6}$ 
$\therefore$ Domain = [1, 6]

questions.question: Let f(x) = x^2 and g(x) = $\sqrt x$, then
questions.question_raw: Let f(x) = x2 and g(x) = {tex}\sqrt x{/tex}, then
questions.explanation:
gof(x) = g(f(x)) = g(x^2) = $\sqrt{x^2}$ = x
$\therefore$ gof(-2) = 2

questions.question: Suppose that g (x) = 1 + $\sqrt x $ and f (g(x)) = 3 + 2$\sqrt x $ + x, then f (x) is
questions.question_raw: Suppose that g (x) = 1 + {tex}\sqrt x {/tex} and f (g(x)) = 3 + 2{tex}\sqrt x {/tex} + x, then f (x) is
questions.explanation:
We have g(x) = 1 + $\sqrt{x}$ and f(g(x)) = 3 + 2$\sqrt{x}$ + x 
$\Rightarrow$ f(1 + $\sqrt{x}$) = 3 + 2$\sqrt{x}$ + x = (1 + $\sqrt{x}$)^2 + 2
$\therefore$ f(x) = x^2 + 2

questions.question: If f : [1, $\infty \infty $) $ \to $ [2, $\infty \infty $) is given by f(x) = x + $\frac{1}{x}$ then f^-1(x) equals]
questions.question_raw: If f : [1, {tex}\infty \infty {/tex}) {tex} \to {/tex} [2, {tex}\infty \infty {/tex}) is given by f(x) = x + {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} then f-1(x) equals]
questions.explanation:
Let y = f(x), then
y = x + $\frac{1}{x}$ $\Rightarrow$ y = $\frac{x^{2}+1}{x}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + 1 = xy $\Rightarrow$ x^2 - xy + 1 = 0 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{y \pm \sqrt{y^{2}-4}}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ f^-1(y) = $\frac{y \pm \sqrt{y^{2}-4}}{2}$ (negative sign is rejected)
f^-1 (x) = $\frac{x+\sqrt{x^{2}-4}}{2}$

questions.question: The minimum value of (x - $\alpha $) (x – $\beta $) is
questions.question_raw: The minimum value of (x - {tex}\alpha {/tex}) (x – {tex}\beta {/tex}) is
questions.explanation:
Let f(x) = (x - $\alpha$)(x - $\beta$) then, 
f'(x) = $(x - \alpha ).1\; + 1.\;(x\; - \beta )$ 
$ \Rightarrow$ f'(x) = 2x - ($\alpha$ + $\beta$) 
$\Rightarrow$ f''(x) = 2
Now, f''(x) = 0 $\Rightarrow$ 2x - $(\alpha+\beta)$ = 0
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}$ 
At x = $\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}$, f''(x) = 2 > 0
$\therefore$ x = $ \frac{{\alpha \; + \beta }}{2}$ is point of minimum value and minimum value is,
$f\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}\right)$ = $\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}-\alpha\right)\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}-\beta\right)$ 
= $\left(\frac{\beta-\alpha}{2}\right)\left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)$ = $-\frac{1}{4}(\alpha-\beta)^{2}$

questions.question: If f (x) = ${(25 - {x^4})^{1/4}}$ for 0 < x <$\sqrt 5 $, then $f\left(f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right)$ =
questions.question_raw: If f (x) = {tex}{(25 - {x^4})^{1/4}}{/tex} for 0 < x <{tex}\sqrt 5 {/tex}, then {tex}f\left(f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right){/tex} =
questions.explanation:
fof(x) = f(f(x)) = $\left\{ {f{{\left( {25 - {x^4}} \right)}^{\frac{1}{4}}}} \right\}$
= $\left\{25-\left(25-x^{4}\right)\right\}^{\frac{1}{4}}$ = $\left(x^{4}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}$ = x 
$\therefore$ $f\left(f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right)$ = $\frac{1}{2}$
= 2^-1

questions.question: If f(x) = $\log \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)$, then f(x) is
questions.question_raw: If f(x) = {tex}\log \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right){/tex}, then f(x) is
questions.explanation:
f(-x) = $\log \left( {\frac{{1 - x}}{{1 + x}}} \right)$ 
= log (1 - x) - log (1 + x)
= -[log (1 + x) - log (1 - x)]

questions.question: Let the function f be defined by f(x) = $\frac{2 x+1}{1-3 x}$, then f^-1(x) is
questions.question_raw: Let the function f be defined by f(x) = {tex}\frac{2 x+1}{1-3 x}{/tex}, then f-1(x) is
questions.explanation:
Let y = f(x), then
y = $\frac{2 x+1}{1-3 x}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ y(1- 3x) = 2x + 1 
$\Rightarrow$ y - 3xy = 2x + 1
$\Rightarrow$ 3xy + 2x = y - 1
$\Rightarrow$ x(3y + 2) = y - 1
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{y-1}{3 y+2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ f^-1(y) = $\frac{y-1}{3 y+2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ f^-1(x) = $\frac{x-1}{3 x+2}$

questions.question: In a city 20 percent of the population travels by car, 50 percent travels by bus and 10 percent travels by both car and bus. Then persons travelling by a car or bus is
questions.question_raw: In a city 20 percent of the population travels by car, 50 percent travels by bus and 10 percent travels by both car and bus. Then persons travelling by a car or bus is
questions.explanation:
Let A denote the set of persons traveling by car, B denotes the set of persons traveling by bus, then
n(A) = 20, n(B) = 50, n(A $\cap$ B) = 10
$\therefore$ n(A $\cup$ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A $\cap$ B)
= 20 + 50 - 10 = 60

questions.question: If g(x) = x^2 + x - 2 and $\frac 1 2$(gof)(x) = 2x^2 - 5x + 2, then f(x) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If g(x) = x2 + x - 2 and {tex}\frac 1 2{/tex}(gof)(x) = 2x2 - 5x + 2, then f(x) is equal to
questions.explanation:
Consider, f(x) = 2x - 3; g(x) = x^2 + x - 2
$\therefore$ gof(x) = g[f(x)] = g[2x - 3]
= (2x - 3)^2 + 2x - 3 - 2
= 4x^2 + 9 - 12x + 12x - 3 - 2
= 4x^2 - 10x + 4
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{1}{2}$gof(x) = 2x^2 - 5x + 2
$\therefore$ f(x) = 2x - 3 is correct.

questions.question: The minimum value of sin x + cos x is
questions.question_raw: The minimum value of sin x + cos x is
questions.explanation:
Let ${f(x)\; = \;\sin \;x + \;cos\;x}$
${\therefore \;f'(x)\; = {\text{co}}s\;x\; - \;\sin \;x}$
${ \Rightarrow \;f''\;(x)\; = \; - \sin \;x\; - \;\cos \;x}$
Now, ${f'(x)\; = 0}$
${ \Rightarrow \;\cos \;x\; - \;\sin \;x\; = \;0\; \Rightarrow \;\sin \;x\; = \;\cos \;x\; \Rightarrow \;\tan \;x\; = \;1}$
${ \Rightarrow \;x\; = n\pi \; + \;\frac{\pi }{4},\;{\text{n}} \in \;{\text{z}}}$
At ${{\text{x}}\; = \pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4},}$
${{\text{f''}}\;({\text{x}})\; = \; - \sin \;\left( {\pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\; - \;\cos \;\;\left( {\pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)}$
${ = \;\sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\; + \cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\; = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\; + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\; = \sqrt {2\;} \;\; > \;0}$
${\therefore \;{\text{x}}\; = \;\pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4}}$ is point of minimum
Minimum value ${ = \;\sin \left( {\pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\;\; + \cos \left( {\pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)}$
${ = \; - \sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\; - \;\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)}$
${ = \; - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\; - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\; = \; - \frac{2}{{\sqrt 2 }}\; = \; - \sqrt 2 }$

questions.question: The relation R = {1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3)} on the set {1, 2, 3) is
questions.question_raw: The relation R = {1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3)} on the set {1, 2, 3) is
questions.explanation:
Given Relation R = {(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3)}
Reflexive: If a relation has {(a, b)} as its element, then it should also have {(a, a), (b, b)} as its elements too.
Symmetric: If a relation has (a, b) as its element, then it should also have {(b, a)} as its element too.
Transitive: If a relation has {(a, b), (b, c)} as its elements, then it should also have {(a,c)} as its element too.
Now, the given relation satisfies all these three properties.
Therefore, its an equivalence relation.

questions.question: In Z, the set of all integers, the inverse of –7 w.r.t. $*$ defined by a * b = a + b + 7 for all a, b $ \in $ Z, is
questions.question_raw: In Z, the set of all integers, the inverse of –7 w.r.t. {tex}*{/tex} defined by a * b = a + b + 7 for all a, b {tex} \in {/tex} Z, is
questions.explanation:
We have, a $\times$ b = a + b + 7
Let e be an identity element then, 
a $\times$ e = a = e $\times$ a
$\Rightarrow$ a $\times$ e = a and e $\times$ a = a
$\Rightarrow$ e = -7 $\in$ z
$\therefore$ -7 is identity elements
Since the inverse of identity elements, e is itself.
$\therefore $ inverse of - 7 = - 7

questions.question: A class has 175 students. The following data shows the number of students opting one or more subject. Maths - 100, Physics - 70, Chemistry - 40, Maths and Physics - 30, Maths and Chemistry - 38, Physics and Chemistry - 23, Maths, Physics and Chemistry - 18. How many have opted for Mathematics alone?
questions.question_raw: A class has 175 students. The following data shows the number of students opting one or more subject. Maths - 100, Physics - 70, Chemistry - 40, Maths and Physics - 30, Maths and Chemistry - 38, Physics and Chemistry - 23, Maths, Physics and Chemistry - 18. How many have opted for Mathematics alone?
questions.explanation:
Let M denote the set of students who had taken mathematics, P the set of students who had taken physics and C the set of students who had taken chemistry. Then we have
n(U) = 175, n(M) = 100, n(P) = 70, n(C) = 40, n(M $\cap$ P) = 30, n(M $\cap$ C) = 38, n(P $\cap$ C) = 23, n(M $\cap$ P $\cap$ C) = 18
$\therefore$ no. of students who opted for mathematics alone = n(M $\cap$ P' $\cap$ C')
= n(M $\cap$ P' $\cap$ C')
= n[M $\cap$ (P $\cap$ C)'] = n(M) - n(M $\cap$ (P $\cup$ C))
= n(M) - n[(M $\cap$ P) $\cup$ (M $\cap$ C)]
= n(M) - n[(M $\cap$ P) $\cup$ (M $\cap$ C)]
= n(M) - [n(M $\cap$ P) + n(M $\cap$ C) - n(M $\cap$ P $\cap$ C)]
= 100 - [30 + 38 - 18]
= 100 - [50] = 50

${\therefore {\text{f}}{\text{x}}) = {{\text{x}}^2} - 2}$

questions.question: Suppose f : [2, 2] $ \to $R be defined by $f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \; - 1\;\;for\;\;\;\;\;\;\; - 2 \leq x \leq 0 \\ x - 1\;for\;\;\;\;\;\;\;0 \leq x \leq 2\;\;\; \\ \end{gathered} \right.,$ Then { x $ \in $ [-2, 2]: x $ \le $ 0 and f (| x |) = x} =
questions.question_raw: Suppose f : [2, 2] {tex} \to {/tex}R be defined by {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \; - 1\;\;for\;\;\;\;\;\;\; - 2 \leq x \leq 0 \\ x - 1\;for\;\;\;\;\;\;\;0 \leq x \leq 2\;\;\; \\ \end{gathered} \right.,{/tex} Then { x  {tex} \in {/tex} [-2, 2]: x {tex} \le {/tex} 0 and f (| x |) = x} =
questions.explanation:
f : [-2, 2] $\rightarrow$ R is defined by
${{\text{f}}({\text{x}})\; = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} { - 1,\;\;\; - 2 \leqslant {\text{x}} \leqslant 2} \\ {{\text{x}} - 1,\;0 \leqslant {\text{x}} \leqslant 2} \end{array}} \right.\;}$
Let x $\leq$ 0 and f(|x|) = x
Now, f(|x|) = x $\Rightarrow$ |x| - 1 = x
${ \Rightarrow - {\text{x}} - 1\; = \;{\text{x}}\;\left[ {\because \;\left| {\text{x}} \right|\; \geqslant \;0} \right]}$

$\therefore\{\mathrm{x} \in[-2,2]: \mathrm{x} \leqslant 0$ and $\mathrm{f}(|\mathrm{x}|=\mathrm{x}\}=\left\{-\frac{1}{2}\right\}$

questions.question: If f : R $\to$ R and g: R $\to$ R are given by f(x) = |x| and g(x) = [x] for each x $ \in $ R, then {x $ \in $ R} : g(f(x_o)) $ \in $ f (g(x))] =

Domain of F = R

questions.question:
Consider the following relations: 1. A - B = A - $(A \cap B)$ 2. A = $(A \cap B)$ $\mathop \cup 
olimits $(A - B). 3. A - (B $\mathop \cup 
olimits $ C) = (A - B) $\mathop \cup 
olimits $ (A - C). Which of these is/are correct?
questions.question_raw: Consider the following relations: 1. A - B = A - {tex}(A \cap B){/tex} 2. A = {tex}(A \cap B){/tex} {tex}\mathop \cup olimits {/tex}(A - B). 3. A - (B {tex}\mathop \cup olimits {/tex} C) = (A - B) {tex}\mathop \cup olimits {/tex} (A - C). Which of these is/are correct?
questions.explanation:
$A - (A \cap B) = A \cap (A \cap B')\,\,[\because A - B = A \cap B']$
 $ = A \cap (A' \cap B')\,$[By De - morgan's law]
 $ = (A \cap A') \cup (A \cap B')\,$
 $ = \phi \cup (A \cap B') = A \cap B' = A - B\,$
 $(A \cap B) \cap (A - B) = (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap B')$
 $ = X \cup (A \cap B')$ Where X = $A \cap B$
 $ = (X \cup A) \cap (X \cup B')$
 $ = {\text{A}} \cap ({\text{A}} \cup {\text{B'}})\left[ {{\text{X}} \cup {\text{A}} = ({\text{A}} \cap {\text{B}}) \cup {\text{A}} = {\text{A}} \cup {\text{B'}}} \right]$ $= {\text{A}}(\because {\text{A}}{\text{B}} \subset {\text{A}}){\text{X}} \cup {\text{B'}} $ $= ({\text{A}} \cap {\text{B}}) \cup {\text{B'}} = ({\text{A}} \cup {\text{B'}}) \cap ({\text{B}} \cup {\text{B'}}) = ({\text{A}} \cup {\text{B'}}) \cap {\text{U}}$
 ${\text{ = A}}\left[ {\because {\text{A}} \subset {\text{A}} \cup {\text{B'}}} \right]$
 This is correct because,
 $A - (B \cup C) = (A - B) \cap (A - C)$

questions.question: Two finite sets have m and n elements. The number of elements in the power set of the first is 48 more than the total number of elements in the power set of the second. Then the values of m and n are
questions.question_raw: Two finite sets have m and n elements. The number of elements in the power set of the first is 48 more than the total number of elements in the power set of the second. Then the values of m and n are
questions.explanation:
Let A has m elements and B gas n elements. Then, no. of elements in
P(A) = 2^m and no. of elements in P(B) = 2^n.]
By the question,
2^m = 2^n + 48
$\Rightarrow $ 2^m - 2^n = 48
This is possible, if 2^m = 64, 2^n = 16. (As 64 - 16 = 48) 
$\therefore$ 2m = 64 $\Rightarrow$ 2^m = 2^6 
$\Rightarrow$ m = 6
Also, 2^4 = 16 $\Rightarrow$ 2^4 = 24
$\Rightarrow$ n = 4

questions.question: Let S be the set of all real numbers. Then the relation R = {(a, b) : 1 + ab > 0} on S is
questions.question_raw: Let S be the set of all real numbers. Then the relation R = {(a, b) : 1 + ab > 0} on S is

options.label: B
options.text: reflexive and symmetric but not transitive.

options.label: C
options.text: symmetric and transitive but not reflexive.

options.label: D
options.text: reflexive and transitive but not symmetric.

correct_option_texts: reflexive and symmetric but not transitive.
questions.explanation:
Reflexive: since 1 + a^2 > 0 $\forall \;{\text{a}} \in {\text{S}}$ 
$ \Rightarrow \;1 + \;{\text{a}},\;{\text{a}} > \;0\forall \;{\text{a}}\; \in \;{\text{S}}$ $\Rightarrow(a, a) \in R \forall a$ 
$ \Rightarrow $ R is reflexive.
Symmetric: Let a, b $ \in $ S Such that (a, b) $ \in $ R, then,
1 + ab > 0 $\Rightarrow$ 1 + ba > 0 $\Rightarrow$ (b, a ) $\in$ R
$ \Rightarrow $ R is symmetric.
Transitivity: Here 1 + 1.2 = 1 + 2 = 3 > 0 
$\Rightarrow$ 1 + (-2)(-3) = 1 + 6 = 7 > 0
$\Rightarrow$ (1, 2), (-2, -3) $\in$ R
Now, 1 + 1(-3) = 1 - 3 = -2 < 0
$\therefore $ (1, -3) $\notin$ R
$\therefore$ R is not a transition.

questions.question: A function f : [0, $\infty $] $ \to $ [0, $\infty $) defined as $f(x) =\frac {x} {1 + x}$ is
questions.question_raw: A function f : [0, {tex}\infty {/tex}] {tex} \to {/tex} [0, {tex}\infty {/tex}) defined as {tex}f(x) =\frac {x} {1 + x}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
${{\text{f}}:\left[ {0,\omega } \right]\; \to \left( {0,\omega } \right)}$ is defined by,
${{\text{f}}({\text{x}})\; = \;\frac{{\text{x}}}{{1 + {\text{x}}}}}$
one - one : ${{\text{f}}({{\text{x}}_1})\; = \;{\text{f}}({{\text{x}}_2})}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{x}_1}{1+\mathrm{x}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_2}{1+\mathrm{x}_2}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}_1\left(1+\mathrm{x}_2\right)=\mathrm{x}_2\left(1+\mathrm{x}_1\right) \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}_1=\mathrm{x}_2$
$ \Rightarrow $ f is one - one
onto: Let ${{\text{y}} \in \left( {0,\omega } \right)}$ (co - domain) be arbitrary, such that f(x) = y
$\Rightarrow \frac{x}{1+x=y}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{1-\mathrm{y}} \notin(0, \omega) \forall \mathrm{y} \in(0, \omega)$
e.g. y = 2 $\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{2}{1-2}=\frac{2}{-1}=-2 \notin(0, \omega)$
$\therefore$ f is not onto

questions.question: If f : [1, $\infty \infty $) $ \to $ [2, $\infty \infty $) is given by f(x) = x + $\frac{1}{x}$ then f^-1(x) equals]
questions.question_raw: If f : [1, {tex}\infty \infty {/tex}) {tex} \to {/tex} [2, {tex}\infty \infty {/tex}) is given by f(x) = x + {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} then f-1(x) equals]
questions.explanation:
Let y = f(x), then
y = x + $\frac{1}{x}$ $\Rightarrow$ y = $\frac{x^{2}+1}{x}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + 1 = xy $\Rightarrow$ x^2 - xy + 1 = 0 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{y \pm \sqrt{y^{2}-4}}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ f^-1(y) = $\frac{y \pm \sqrt{y^{2}-4}}{2}$ (negative sign is rejected)
f^-1 (x) = $\frac{x+\sqrt{x^{2}-4}}{2}$

questions.question: A function f from the natural numbers to the set of integers defined by f(n) = $ \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{n - 1}}{2},\;\;when\;n\;is\;odd \\ - \frac{n}{2},\;\;\;\;when\;n\;is\;even \\ \end{gathered} \right.$ is
questions.question_raw: A function f from the natural numbers to the set of integers defined by f(n) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{n - 1}}{2},\;\;when\;n\;is\;odd \\ - \frac{n}{2},\;\;\;\;when\;n\;is\;even \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
F(n) = {$\frac{n-1}{2}$, when n is odd; $\frac{-n}{2}$, when n is even 
one - one : Let n_1, n_2 $\in$ N
Case I: n_1 is even, n_2 is even 
$\therefore$ f(n_1) - f(n_2) $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{-n_{1}}{2}=\frac{-n_{2}}{2}$ $\Rightarrow$ n_1 = n_2
Case II: n_1 is odd, n_2 is odd 
$\therefore$ f(n_1) - f(n_2) $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{-n-1}{2}=\frac{-n-2}{2}$ $\Rightarrow$ n_1 = n_2 
Case III: n_1 is even, n_2 is odd 
$\therefore f({n_1}) - \frac{{ - {n_1}}}{2} = $ even as n_1 is even
$\therefore $ f(n_2) is odd, $\frac{{{n_2} - 1}}{2}$ = even as n_2 is odd
But f(n_1) takes values, -1, -2, -3,..... f(n^2) take values, 0, 1, 2, 3,.....
$\therefore {n_1} \ne {n_2} \Rightarrow f({n_1}) \ne f({n_2})$ 
Similarly n, is odd, n_2, is even, then $\therefore {n_1} \ne {n_2} \Rightarrow f({n_1}) \ne f({n_2})$ 
$\Rightarrow $ f is one - one
onto: f(n) = {$\frac{n-1}{2}$, when n is odd; $\frac{-n}{2}$, when n is even} 
$\therefore$ f(1) = 0, f(2) = 1, f(5) = 2, f(7) = 3, f(9) - 4, ... f(2)= -1, f(4) = -2, f(6) = -3, f(8) = -4, ......
$\therefore$ Range of f = {......, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ....) = z
$\therefore$ F is onto

questions.question: Domain of definition of the function f(x) = $\frac{3}{4-x^2}+\log _{10}\left(x^3-x\right)$ is
questions.question_raw: Domain of definition of the function f(x) = {tex}\frac{3}{4-x^2}+\log _{10}\left(x^3-x\right){/tex} is
questions.explanation:
For f(x) to be real, we must have
4 - x^2 $ \ne$ 0 and x^3 - x > 0
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 $ \ne$ 4 and x(x^2 - 1) > 0
$\Rightarrow$ x $ \ne$ 2, -2 and x(x - 1)(x + 1) > 0
$\Rightarrow$ x $ \ne$ 2, -2 and -1 < x < 0, 1 < x < $\infty$
$\therefore$ Domain = (-1, 0) $\cup$ (1, 2) $\cup$ (2, $\infty$)

questions.question: If f : $R \to R$ satisfies f (x + y) = f (x ) + f (y) for all x, y $ \in $ R and f (1) = 7, then $\sum\limits_{r = 1}^n {f(r)} $ is
questions.question_raw: If f : {tex}R \to R{/tex} satisfies f (x + y) = f (x ) + f (y) for all x, y {tex} \in {/tex} R and f (1) = 7, then {tex}\sum\limits_{r = 1}^n {f(r)} {/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Given f(x+y) = f(x) + f(y).....(i) and f(1) = 7
Put x=1, y=1 in equation (i), we obtain quad
f(1+1) = f(1) + f(1) = 14 $\Rightarrow$ f(2) = 14
Similarly f(1 + 1 + 1) = f(2) + f(1) = 14 + 7 $\Rightarrow$ f(3) = 21
Since we have f(1) = 1 $\times$ 7 = 7, f(2) = 2 $\times$ 7 = 14, f(3) = 3 $\times$ 7 = 21,........
we can get f(n) = n $\times$ 7 = 7n
Now $\sum \limits_{r=1}^{n}$ f(r) = f(1) + f(2) + f(3) + ..... + f(n)
7^r=1 + 14 + 21 + ..... + 7n
= 7[1 + 2 + 3 + ..... + n] = 7 $\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$
